summary: Gruber analyzes reports that Apple is in talks with Microsoft to replace Google as the default iPhone search engine. He argues the discussions reflect not just the Apple-Google rivalry but also shrewd business negotiation — Apple needs leverage to extract better terms from Google. He notes default search placement is worth tens of millions annually and growing with iPhone adoption. Gruber expresses hope that Bing succeeds as a Google competitor simply because monopolies are unhealthy. He highlights a buried detail: Apple reportedly has a 'skunk works' exploring its own search product, which serves as leverage whether true or not.
key_points: Default search placement on iPhone and Safari is worth tens of millions per year to Apple, and the value is growing rapidly with iPhone adoption
Apple's negotiations with Microsoft over Bing are basic negotiating leverage — you can't get the best deal from Google without a credible alternative
Bing is the last major search competitor standing after Yahoo agreed to be powered by Bing, making search competition fragile
Apple reportedly has an internal 'skunk works' exploring its own search product, which serves as leverage in negotiations regardless of whether it's real
The leaked negotiations themselves are strategic — Apple wants Google to know it has options
money_quotes: Say what you want about Microsoft in general and Bing in particular, but the fact is, Bing is the only major competitor to Google left standing.
I don't think it's ever healthy, in any market, for one champion to obliterate all competitors.
Even if what Apple ultimately wants is to stick with Google as their default search provider, how can Apple negotiate the best possible terms from Google if they don't have any other option?
So of course Apple is going to negotiate with Microsoft. And of course Apple is going to happily leak the existence of such negotiations to the press.
Apple isn't going to outsource the future.
Which is intriguing if true, and helps Apple's negotiations with both Microsoft and Google even if it isn't.
key_insight: Apple's negotiations with Microsoft over Bing are less about actually switching search providers and more about creating leverage to extract better terms from Google — classic negotiation strategy made public on purpose.
